insights: Incident assignments increased at a statistically significant rate of +6.40 incidents/month (R²=0.96, p<0.0001), accelerating from 2 incidents/month in January 2023 to 77 incidents/month in January 2024 — a 285% increase between first- and second-half averages.

All five user agents experienced statistically significant growth in assigned incidents, with Beth Anglin (+1.31/month, +1800%) and Fred Luddy (+1.30/month, +1400%) showing the steepest proportional increases despite starting from a balanced baseline workload distribution.

Network category incidents (52% of total volume) are the dominant driver of the upward trend, contributing 3.35 additional incidents per month — accounting for over half of the overall monthly growth (+6.40) and ~174.3 of the cumulative increase observed across the period.

Workload distribution among agents is shifting: Charlie Whitherspoon’s share of assignments increased by +8.4 percentage points between early and recent 3-month windows, while Howard Johnson and Luke Wilson each lost share (−2.9% and −4.3%, respectively), indicating emerging concentration risk.

The acceleration in assignment volume is not matched by proportional growth in resolution capacity — average resolution time remains stable at 7.76 days, suggesting rising backlog pressure or diminishing per-agent throughput as volume surges.

The sustained growth period in late 2023 coincides with the sharpest month-over-month increases (>20%), marking it as the highest-risk operational window where resource strain most likely intensified.
summary: The investigation confirms a severe and accelerating increase in incident assignments — growing 285% between halves and reaching 77 incidents/month by early 2024 — directly impacting all five user agents. While initially balanced, workload distribution is now shifting toward Charlie Whitherspoon, and Beth Anglin and Fred Luddy face the steepest proportional growth, signaling potential burnout or capacity saturation risks. Network category incidents alone drive over half the trend, revealing a single fault domain as the primary source of pressure. With resolution times unchanged at ~7.8 days, this surge implies mounting backlogs rather than improved throughput. Next focus should be on root-cause analysis of Network category escalation patterns and targeted capacity assessment for the highest-growth agents.
